name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger) is the world's largest oilfield services company, providing technology and services for reservoir characterization, drilling, production, and processing to the oil and gas industry worldwide. The company operates through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. The 2024 ChampionX acquisition expanded its production chemicals portfolio. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB has executed meaningful business model transformation over the past five years, completing the ChampionX acquisition ($838M quarterly revenue contribution) to expand into production chemicals, rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB to signal a broader energy technology identity, and building the Lumi data and AI platform as a new digital revenue stream [1]. The New Energy division (carbon capture, geothermal, hydrogen) represents a genuine strategic pivot toward energy transition, distinguishing SLB from pure oilfield services peers. AI Deployment (1/2): SLB launched the Lumi data and AI platform — an AI-powered solution designed to optimize drilling operations, enhance subsurface characterization, and drive efficiency across the energy sector [3]. The platform embeds AI into client-facing workflows with measurable operational improvements. However, specific AI revenue figures are not disclosed. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the #1 global oilfield services company by revenue ($35.7B), with dominant positions in international markets, reservoir characterization, and digital solutions. However, international revenue declined 4% in Q1 2026 due to Middle East disruptions, and excluding ChampionX, organic revenue decreased 7% YoY [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Lumi AI platform, New Energy ventures (carbon capture, geothermal), and integrated performance contracts represent pipeline advancement, though specific next-generation product launches with timelines are not well-defined. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending is among the highest in oilfield services, supporting the Lumi platform, automation, and New Energy R&D [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): SLB's purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is specific and thoughtful, reflecting genuine intentionality in connecting oilfield technology to the energy transition challenge. The rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB and the creation of a dedicated New Energy division demonstrate that leadership has aligned strategic decisions to this purpose [3]. However, the purpose has not yet been tested through a major crisis or leadership transition under this articulation — CEO Le Peuch took the helm in 2019 and the rebrand occurred in 2022. Community Impact (1/2): SLB publishes detailed sustainability reports with emissions reduction commitments (net-zero by 2050) and has integrated sustainability metrics into executive compensation. The company has a low ESG controversy score (1.0 on Sustainalytics) and strong MSCI ESG rating of AA [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): SLB ranks #1 in Overall Culture Score on Comparably versus competitors, with employees citing knowledge and technical innovation as cultural strengths [3]. CEO Le Peuch has provided stable leadership since 2019. However, office culture and professional development are cited as improvement areas, and the oilfield services industry inherently has high field turnover. Insider Buying (1/2): Mixed insider activity — Director de la Chevardiere sold $109K in May 2026 and Officer Steve Gassen sold $3.0M. Only stock award grants offset sales. No meaningful net insider buying identified [1].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a dividend stock. D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target, reflecting conservative leverage [5]. ROE of 14.1% is marginally below the 15% target. Revenue declined 1.6% YoY (FY2024 to FY2025), though Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B grew 3% YoY — however, excluding ChampionX, organic revenue fell 7% due to Middle East conflict disruptions [1]. The 3-year revenue CAGR of 8.3% is solid but below the 10% target. Forward P/E of 17.1x is reasonable for the sector leader. Profit margin of 9.3% is below the 20% target, reflecting the services business model. Dividend metrics are strong — payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target but sustainable, with 5 consecutive years of payment and growth since the 2020 reset, and an impressive 22.9% 5-year dividend growth CAGR as the company has rebuilt from the 2020 cut [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business — providing physical oilfield services, subsurface characterization, well construction, and production systems — is structurally immune to AI substitution. Drilling wells, deploying completion equipment, and manufacturing production hardware require physical assets, trained field crews, and on-site execution that AI cannot replicate [1][3]. The company's 109,000-employee global workforce operates heavy equipment in remote locations across 100+ countries. Regulatory requirements for well safety, environmental compliance, and reservoir management create additional barriers to AI disruption. AI actually increases demand for SLB's services by enabling more sophisticated well placement and reservoir optimization. The primary risk is that AI-driven efficiency could reduce the number of wells drilled per unit of production, but this is offset by AI enabling access to more complex reservoirs. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB generates AI-enabled revenue through the Lumi data and AI platform, which provides AI-powered subsurface modeling, drilling optimization, and real-time operational insights to E&P clients. The platform is commercially deployed and represents a growing digital revenue stream. The Digital & Integration division generated $3.9B in FY2025 revenue, with AI capabilities embedded across the product suite [1][3].

infinite_game.summary: SLB under CEO Olivier Le Peuch has demonstrated stronger infinite game characteristics than most energy services peers — the strategic rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB, the creation of a New Energy division for carbon capture and geothermal, and the articulation of 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' reflect genuine long-term thinking. The company is positioning for the energy transition rather than just optimizing the current hydrocarbon cycle, though it remains to be seen whether this commitment will survive a sustained downturn.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): 'Driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' is a genuine cause that connects SLB's technology capabilities to the larger challenge of energy transition. The rebrand from Schlumberger, creation of the New Energy division (carbon capture, geothermal, hydrogen), and integration of sustainability metrics into executive compensation demonstrate that strategic decisions trace back to this purpose [3][4]. However, the cause was articulated only in 2022 under Le Peuch — it has not been tested through a leadership transition or major crisis. SLB was Schlumberger for 96 years before this identity shift, creating questions about durability.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): SLB ranks #1 in Overall Culture Score on Comparably versus competitors, with employees valuing the technical knowledge and innovation-driven culture [3]. CEO Le Peuch has provided stable leadership since 2019, and the executive team has maintained continuity through the rebrand and ChampionX integration. However, office culture and professional development are cited as areas needing improvement. The 109,000-employee global organization spans diverse field and office environments where trust levels vary significantly.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB competes with Halliburton and Baker Hughes while maintaining an infinite competitive posture — the company frames its strategy around technology differentiation and energy transition leadership rather than market share destruction. Le Peuch's commentary focuses on building the industry's most advanced digital and AI capabilities rather than 'beating' specific competitors. The New Energy investments reflect using competitive dynamics to advance innovation rather than zero-sum thinking [1][3].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB and creation of the New Energy division (carbon capture, geothermal, hydrogen) represent meaningful strategic evolution — the company is signaling willingness to move beyond pure oilfield services toward broader energy technology [3]. The ChampionX acquisition expanded into production chemicals. However, New Energy remains a small fraction of total revenue, and the core business is still overwhelmingly hydrocarbon-focused. This is incremental evolution rather than transformative reinvention.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Le Peuch demonstrated courage by rebranding a 96-year-old company (Schlumberger to SLB), investing in New Energy ventures that may not generate returns for years, and integrating sustainability into executive compensation [3][4]. The ChampionX acquisition was a bold capital allocation bet. However, the company continues to generate 95%+ of revenue from traditional hydrocarbon services, and the New Energy investments are modest relative to total capital deployment — these are hedged bets rather than costly sacrifices.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest oilfield services company with $35.7B in FY2025 revenue, trading at a forward P/E of 17.1x with a 2.06% dividend yield and 22.9% 5-year dividend growth CAGR [1]. The company's strategic positioning is differentiated from peers — the Lumi AI platform, New Energy division, and ChampionX integration create a broader energy technology franchise that should command premium valuation. D/E of 42.5% provides balance sheet flexibility, and the Digital & Integration division ($3.9B revenue) offers higher-margin digital revenue growth. Q1 2026 revenue grew 3% YoY to $8.72B, with North American revenue surging 26% on land and offshore activity recovery.
thesis_continued: The primary concern is the organic revenue decline. Excluding ChampionX, Q1 2026 revenue fell 7% YoY as Middle East conflict disrupted international operations — international revenue declined 4% to $6.47B [1]. GAAP EPS of $0.50 declined 14% YoY, and profit margin of 9.3% is structurally low for the services business model. The PEG ratio of 1.97 suggests the stock may be overvalued relative to near-term growth. Payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target, creating less room for dividend growth if earnings decline further. The energy transition remains a long-term structural risk — while SLB's New Energy investments mitigate this more than peers, hydrocarbon services still represent 95%+ of revenue, and a sustained decline in global drilling activity would significantly impact the business [4]. Geopolitical risk is elevated given SLB's dependence on international markets (74% of revenue).
key_catalysts: Middle East geopolitical stabilization could reverse the 4% international revenue decline and unlock pent-up drilling demand in SLB's largest market
Lumi AI platform adoption accelerating digital revenue growth within the Digital & Integration division, driving margin expansion beyond the current 9.3% profit margin

ChampionX integration synergies flowing through — the $838M quarterly revenue contribution should expand as cross-selling production chemicals with existing well construction and reservoir clients scales
key_risks: Organic revenue decline of 7% (excluding ChampionX) in Q1 2026 could persist if Middle East disruptions continue and global drilling activity weakens, compressing margins further
PEG ratio of 1.97x and trailing P/E of 25.2x suggest the stock is priced for a recovery that may not materialize if oil prices weaken or OPEC+ production decisions reduce upstream investment

International revenue concentration (74%) creates elevated geopolitical risk — operations in the Middle East, Russia (exited), and Latin America are subject to political instability, sanctions, and regulatory uncertainty
sources: SLB Q1 2026 Earnings Release and 10-Q — SEC Filing, April 2026
SLB FY2025 Annual Report and Q4 2025 Earnings — SEC Form 8-K, January 2026
SLB AI Strategy, Lumi Platform, Leadership, and Culture — AI Magazine, Comparably, OGR Energy, 2024-2026
SLB ESG Commitments and Sustainability — Sustainalytics (AA rating), KnowESG, Offshore Technology, 2022-2026
Yahoo Finance — SLB Key Statistics and Financial Data, accessed May 2026
